metadata.title: ReferMe: Ameya Padwad's Referral Automation Tool
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Ameya Padwad
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Ameya Padwad — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. ReferMe automates referrals, letting users log sends and responses to track outreach. Its real metric is message quality: do the messages actually get responses? Prof Bear flags the pitch as handwavy, describing the final vision instead of the 30-day build. By Week 4 the work is basic job scraping, because discovery does not yet equal access.

artifactLines: ReferMe automates referrals, letting users log sends and responses to track outreach.
Its real metric is message quality: do the messages actually get responses?
Prof Bear flags the pitch as handwavy, describing the final vision instead of the 30-day build.
By Week 4 the work is basic job scraping, because discovery does not yet equal access.

beats.narration_text: Prof Bear's note that the pitch gets 'handwavy', describing the final vision instead of the 30-day build, while Week 4 is really just basic job scraping, is the scoping problem I want to fix. Help me: (1) separate ReferMe's end-state vision from what a 30-day MVP can honestly ship; (2) explain why message quality and response rates are the right early success metric; (3) redesign the Week 4 scraping milestone into something that proves discovery converts to actual access.
